content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The honest answer is yes. The more useful answer is it depends entirely on things most people do not think about before they start building.
I have been running an automated trading bot on Polymarket for about 90 days. Not paper trading. Not backtesting. Real money, real fills, real losses on the bad days. Here is what the data actually shows.
The strategy is market making on BTC 5 minute and 15 minute price prediction markets. The bot posts on both sides of the order book and collects the spread when both sides fill. No price prediction involved. No guessing whether BTC goes up or down. Just sitting between buyers and sellers and getting paid the difference.
Day one I thought this would be straightforward. Post orders, collect spread, repeat. What I did not understand is that the spread only exists when there is enough volume to fill both sides. On a dead market your orders just sit there. Nothing fills. The bot looks busy in the logs and makes absolutely nothing.
The first month was mostly figuring out when not to trade. BTC 5 minute markets have a pattern that took me three weeks of log data to see clearly. The order book is basically empty for the first two minutes of every market. Thin volume, wide spreads, almost no counterparties on either side. Then the last three minutes happen and everything changes. Volume spikes. Both sides of the book fill in. The probability is collapsing toward resolution and traders are committing. That is the only window where the strategy actually works.
Once I understood the timing my fill rate went up immediately without changing anything else about the strategy.
The second month I got caught by a volatility problem. BTC went into a flat range for about four hours one night. Almost no price movement. My bot kept posting orders on 5 minute markets that kept opening and closing with almost no volume. By morning I had a small but real loss from a session where the bot ran perfectly by every technical measure. No errors. No crashes. Just trading in conditions where the strategy had no edge.
That forced me to build a volatility filter. Before the bot enters any market now it checks recent price movement against a threshold. If BTC is moving less than 0.3 percent across the last five candles the market gets skipped entirely. That single change improved weekly P&L more than any other optimization I made.
The third month was the first month that felt like the strategy was actually working as intended. Volatility filter running, timing logic solid, position limits in place to stop inventory accumulating on one side when fills are uneven.
So what does 90 days of data actually show?
The strategy makes money in the right conditions. High volatility BTC sessions, active markets, good volume. On those days the bot runs cleanly and P&L is positive. On slow days where BTC barely moves the bot now sits idle and waits. That is the correct behavior. Idle and flat is better than active and losing.
The Rust rewrite helped more than I expected. I started in TypeScript at around 48 milliseconds average execution time. Moved the execution engine to Rust and got under one millisecond. On a 90 second volume window that difference in speed translated directly into better fills. Same strategy, same logic, meaningfully better results just from being faster.
The biggest lesson across 90 days is not about the code at all. It is about knowing when your strategy has edge and when it does not. A bot that trades in every condition will lose money in the conditions where it should not be trading. A bot that only trades when the setup is right will make less total trades and more total profit.
If you are thinking about building something like this the Polymarket CLOB API is genuinely good. Real order book, real limit orders, real fills. The infrastructure is there. The hard part is building the logic around when to use it and when to walk away.
All my code is open source at github.com/casatrick if you want to see exactly how any of this is implemented.
